text: Well, welcome to Reflux UK. Today we're showing you a video of a patient with quite a large hiatus hernia who's going to undergo a LINX implantation.
The video displays a visible defect in the hiatus at the center of the screen, indicating the presence of a large hiatus hernia.
And this is the cause of their significant reflux symptoms with heartburn and regurgitation affecting their quality of life.
The laparoscopic camera provides a view of the abdominal cavity as the surgeon begins the keyhole procedure for hernia repair.

The surgeon begins to dissect the stomach from surrounding tissues, visible at the bottom left of the screen, in preparation to open the hiatus and expose the esophagus due to the large hiatus hernia.

The video shows the stomach protruding into the chest cavity through an enlarged hiatus, illustrating the anatomical defect associated with a large hiatus hernia. The stomach's upward movement compromises the valve mechanism, leading to reflux symptoms.
The surgeon mobilizes the stomach by gently dividing small blood vessels and the peritoneal reflection on the side, with the left crus muscle visible in the center of the laparoscopic view.

The surgeon dissects the esophagus from the surrounding crura, which encircle the esophagus and form the hiatus of the diaphragm. The esophagus is visible in the center of the screen as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon moves to the right side of the surgical field, dividing the connective tissue between the esophagus and the liver, visible on the left side of the screen.
The surgeon positions the esophagus on the left side of the screen and the stomach on the right side, preparing for further dissection and mobilization.

Now this procedure is all done as a keyhole operation and the patient chose to have a LINX implantation because he wanted to avoid the side effects which can affect patients undergoing traditional fundoplication.

And when we started doing LINX surgery we tended to exclude patients with hiatus hernias but over the years we've learned that actually the LINX works very well in these patients and there's no reason whatsoever, in fact there's every reason why they should undergo surgery in the same way as you would perform a fundoplication on these patients should they have persistent symptoms which don't respond to the normal treatments with diet modification and
with antacid medications.
The surgeon divides the connective tissue at the right crus of the diaphragm, separating it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon reveals the oesophagus in the center of the screen, with the posterior vagus nerve visible as a white band just below it.
The surgeon gently separates the surrounding tissue to visually identify the oesophagus, revealing its anatomical position within the surgical field.
The surgeon has mobilized the left, right, and anterior aspects of the hernia site and is now proceeding to mobilize the posterior aspect.
The surgeon visualizes the oesophagus at the front and the anterior vagus nerve. The dissection has progressed around the back of the oesophagus, revealing its posterior aspect.
The surgeon ensures that the oesophagus and stomach are positioned correctly below the diaphragm, preventing the stomach from migrating back into the chest, which could lead to recurrent symptoms.

The surgeon mobilizes the bottom of the oesophagus, ensuring it sits well below the crura. The crura are visible encircling the oesophagus, which is positioned from the top left to the bottom right of the screen. The surgeon gently manipulates the oesophagus, preparing to create a window between the posterior vagus nerve and the oesophagus for the placement of the LINX device.
The surgeon holds the vagus nerve with the left hand while a window is visible between the nerve and the oesophagus, prepared for the placement of the LINX device.
The surgeon has fully mobilized the esophagus and created a window between the posterior vagus nerve and the esophagus, preparing for the placement of the LINX device.

The surgeon holds the esophagus up to reveal a large gap around it, indicating an enlarged hiatus in the diaphragm. The next step involves repairing the crura to ensure the hiatus fits snugly around the esophagus.

The surgeon prepares to perform a crural repair to ensure the lower esophageal valve functions properly. The size of the hiatal defect will determine the number of stitches needed to close the gap around the esophagus.

The surgeon places the first non-absorbable stitch to repair the crura, ensuring the esophagus will sit snugly within the diaphragm. The stitch is visible as it secures the tissue, contributing to the larger repair needed for this patient.
The surgical team uses three operating ports for the procedure: one for the camera providing the visual field, and two smaller 5mm ports for the instruments used in the hernia repair.

The surgeon places four sutures through the camera port to repair the crura, but a visible gap remains around the esophagus. A fifth stitch is added to ensure the hiatus is adequately closed for optimal valve function, addressing the large hiatus hernia in the patient.

The surgeon assesses the tension of the repaired crura around the esophagus, ensuring it is snug but not overly tight to avoid postoperative swallowing difficulties. The goal is to achieve a balance that supports effective valve function.
The surgeon has completed placing five sutures inside the abdomen for the hernia repair. The sutures are visible and will now be removed as the operation nears completion.

The surgeon implants the LINX device around the oesophagus, selecting the appropriate size from four available options to ensure it fits snugly without compressing the oesophagus. A special measuring device is used to tailor the fit, enhancing the valve function.

The LINX device is positioned around the oesophagus, and the surgeon incrementally tightens it, ensuring no fat or other tissue is between the device and the oesophagus. The measuring device indicates the correct size needed for the implant.
and again it's really important that you don't put one in that's too loose or it won't work or one that's too tight which equally could cause pressure on the oesophagus which one needs to avoid.

So we've put the LINX device in and this is the last bit now so the operation in total although this video is edited only took thirty five minutes from beginning to end and the patient actually came in on the morning of the surgery and the operation was performed at about eleven o'clock and they went home at about five o'clock in the evening so all of these patients will go home on the same day as their operation.
That's one of the beauties of this minimally invasive procedure is that people do get over it very quickly. And apart from the sewing, it's really a pretty straightforward procedure to perform.

The LINX device is positioned behind the oesophagus, passing through a window created between the oesophageal wall and the posterior vagus nerve. Temporary stitches are used to manipulate the device into place, followed by securing it with a specialized three-dimensional lock.

The surgeon confirms the LINX device is securely locked around the oesophagus, ensuring it cannot be pulled apart. The operation concludes with the removal of surgical ports and the retractor used to hold the liver aside, followed by the reversal of anesthesia.

So we can see here this is just the trickiest bit of the operation sometimes, just making sure that we lock it and that it's not going to come apart. and once that's done here we go that's the end of the operation and we'll keep you updated as to how the patient does
The surgeon attempts to separate the two locking beads of the LINX device to ensure it is securely locked in place behind the esophagus.
